title: podman 5.7.0 can't restart a container because the port is already in use... By itself.
body:
### Issue Description

I've got a podman container named `project-579e8dbd` running with port binding `54648-54648:8000-8000`, with `caddy` pointing `https://localhost/projects/579e8dbd` to port `http://localhost:54648` (so `caddy` owns port 80, but the reverse proxy for port 54648 is just a redirect, `podman` "owns" that port)

I then run `podman container restart -t 0 project-579e8dbd` at some point in order to restart it.

Instead of doing so, podman errors out with:

```
Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Failed to bind port 54648 (Address already in use) for option '-t 54648-54648:8000-8000'
```

So really, three things: 

1. what the heck is "pasta", I am talking to "podman", just report as podman with an error code that actually means something (e.g. the code itself tells you which tool, and where, things went wrong =)
2. yes, that port is in use... _because you (podman) own it_ so it looks like podman's forgetting to either update or release and recycle the port binding =D
3. that's not the arguments I gave it. I gave it `-t 0`, not `-t` followed by the port bindings (so the error reporter seems to be gobbling strings?)

And as extra data point, omitting the `-t 0` does not change the behaviour here other than changing the error message to not include `-t`  in the port listing.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Create a dockerfile:

```
FROM alpine:latest

# this should speak for itself
RUN apk add --update --no-cache \
    alpine-sdk \
    curl \
    nodejs-lts \
    npm


# just in case
RUN rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*

# link up Node
RUN ln -sf node /usr/bin/nodejs

# for universal convenience, install "http-server" 
RUN npm install -g http-server

# set up a user so we don't run as root
RUN adduser -D -h /home/app -s /bin/ash app
USER app
WORKDIR app

# set up our port and port-based healthcheck
ENV PORT=8000
EXPOSE 8000
HEALTHCHECK --start-period=30s --interval=5m CMD curl -f http://127.0.0.1:8000 || exit 1
```

Then build that image, and run it with a mount binding for a dir that contains the following `run.sh`:

```
npx http-server -c-1 -p $PORT --cors
```

using the following run instruction:

```
podman run --detach --rm --stop-timeout 0 --name project-579e8dbd --mount type=bind,src=./project-579e8dbd,dst=/app -p 54648:8000  project-579e8dbd /bin/sh ./run.sh
```

(obviously, update the paths to match reality)

And then issue the restart:

```
podman container restart -t 0 project-579e8dbd
```

### Describe the results you received

something called "pasta" errors out, claiming the port binding is unavailable, even though it's literally podman's own port binding.

### Describe the results you expected

Same as docker: the container restarts. No questions, no errors, just a restart.

### podman info output


### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional information

comments.body: can you reproduce this problem outside this container image? like simply with nginx or some such ?

I'm not sure what you're asking - I have a container running, I ask podman to restart the container, it errors out citing the container's port is in use (which it was: by podman). I'm not sure I understand how I would reproduce that behaviour without having a container running, and asking podman to restart it...? Especially given that if I redo the process but with docker instead of podman (e.g. build with docker, run with docker, then  restart with docker, using literally the same command arguments but using `docker` instead of `podman`), there are no errors.

To investigate, I suggest you stop the container and check if that port is still in use. If it is, which process is using it? (I expect `wslrelay.exe`). If nothing is using the port on the Windows side, you can use `podman machine ssh` to check what is holding the port inside the machine.

If you are using WSL as the hypervisor, it is possible that WSL keeps listening on that port. I have experienced this myself. The only solution was running `wsl --shutdown` (WARNING: stop the podman machine before doing this to avoid issues) or reinstalling Windows (I upgraded from Windows 10 to 11, which solved the issue).

If the port is occupied by wslrelay or other WSL components, we are unable to resolve the issue.

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

As far as I can tell, the port immediately becomes available: if I use `podman stop` and then I immediately curl the associated localhost server, curl goes `curl (7) failed to connect to host`, and if I run the following "compound" command, where I stop the container and then as immediately as the OS will let me, start a new one on the exact same port, things just work. No errors, just a "restart" by manually stopping and starting:

```
> podman run --detach --rm --stop-timeout 0 --name project-name --mount type=bind,src=.\\content\\project-name,dst=/app -p 63725:8000  project-name /bin/sh .container/run.sh
project-name 
> podman stop project-name && podman run --detach --rm --stop-timeout 0 --name project-name --mount type=bind,src=.\\content\\project-name,dst=/app -p 63725:8000  project-name /bin/sh .container/run.sh
project-name 
```

So the first run instruction starts a container, binds the port, all is well and we get the name for the just-started container, then we fire the `stop && run` which stops the container, then starts a new container on the same port and reports that all was well by showing the container name again, as expected.

And just to confirm it's still doing the wrong thing on restart, 

```
> podman run --detach --rm --stop-timeout 0 --name project-name --mount type=bind,src=.\\content\\project-name,dst=/app -p 63725:8000  project-name /bin/sh .container/run.sh
project-name 
> podman restart -t 0 project-name
Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Failed to bind port 63725 (Address already in use) for option '-t 63725-63725:8000-8000'
```

So it's possible `podman restart` either doesn't wait quite long enough to recycle the port, or maybe there's an async timing issue where it's trying to use the port before a competing codepath had the chance to release it?

I was not able to reproduce this with this commands on main on f42:

```
~ podman run -dt --name webserver -p 8081:80 quay.io/libpod/banner 
17d6fa0fe73908f334f18d7d21596dd10c8f20521717f38834232051cbc468eb
 ~ curl localhost:8081                                             
   ___           __
  / _ \___  ___/ /_ _  ___ ____
 / ___/ _ \/ _  /  ' \/ _ `/ _ \
/_/   \___/\_,_/_/_/_/\_,_/_//_/

~ podman restart webserver 
webserver
 ~ curl localhost:8081                                             
   ___           __
  / _ \___  ___/ /_ _  ___ ____
 / ___/ _ \/ _  /  ' \/ _ `/ _ \
/_/   \___/\_,_/_/_/_/\_,_/_//_/

```

I don't have a Windows machine available to check if this works on Windows. Can you try to reproduce this on your machine with this simple reproducer? Also, could you experiment with different `-t` values?

I suspect that your app is receiving a SIGKILL and isn't stopping gracefully, so the system doesn't have time to release all resources for an immediate restart.

Same error on Windows:
```
> podman run -dt --name webserver -p 8081:80 quay.io/libpod/banner && curl localhost:8081

Trying to pull quay.io/libpod/banner:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob sha256:92ec11331c384eaa113ba801fee97e95d50cb4569ca792b14eef34c996adf381
Copying blob sha256:2408cc74d12b6cd092bb8b516ba7d5e290f485d3eb9672efc00f0583730179e8
Copying blob sha256:64dc81575282d498f0b8f556909cfa5487c2d0a6cad7d88c7e66a6500d0a5c6d
Copying blob sha256:ef4966331ce5628e3b636968bfc3560acf4993043a8e226369007f58173f33d8
Copying config sha256:5ba9aec95f0f3f2a3ff3f76dd5a571519f34884adc6a98b8ef99aadd671a1768
Writing manifest to image destination
c25d281291f800617495b214b496cb9cdf069d5023eaf2d75adf9aa590500009
   ___          __
  / _ \___  ___/ /_ _  ___ ____
 / ___/ _ \/ _  /  ' \/ _ `/ _ \
/_/   \___/\_,_/_/_/_/\_,_/_//_/


> podman restart webserver -t 0 && curl localhost:8081

Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Failed to bind port 8081 (Address already in use) for option '-t 8081-8081:80-80'
```

Same if we don't bother with `-t`:

```
> podman run -dt --name webserver -p 8081:80 quay.io/libpod/banner && curl localhost:8081
f861c4f6add963e85b33fabe4b5c226cfc6131be09407cf73311e78f2ac5999a
   ___          __
  / _ \___  ___/ /_ _  ___ ____
 / ___/ _ \/ _  /  ' \/ _ `/ _ \
/_/   \___/\_,_/_/_/_/\_,_/_//_/


> podman restart webserver

Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Failed to bind port 8081 (Address already in use) for option '-t 8081-8081:80-80'
```

That last one is _very_ weird: no `-t` was provided, why does the error show `-t 8081-8081:80-80`, which isn't how you specify port bindings (because you can't specify port bindings for `restart`)?

As for not having access to a windows machine: I _think_ github actions include Windows and MacOs runners? (if https://github.com/actions/runner-images?tab=readme-ov-file#available-images is still accurate at least) so that might be worth adding to the CI matrix/workflow set?

> As for not having access to a windows machine: I _think_ github actions include Windows and MacOs runners? (if https://github.com/actions/runner-images?tab=readme-ov-file#available-images is still accurate at least) so that might be worth adding to the CI matrix/workflow set?

Standard GitHub Actions runners (Windows/MacOS) cannot be used because our tests require hardware-level nested virtualization to launch the Podman machine (VM). Additionally, running our tests inside a pre-existing container environment creates isolation layers that block the low-level kernel features (cgroups/namespaces) we need to verify.

I tested this on my Windows machine and was able to reproduce it. I think the issue is caused by the slow deallocation of the port by `wslrelay`.

cc @l0rd

I got an easier reproducer:

```bash
podman run --name httpd --rm -d -p 8080:80 httpd && sleep 5 && podman restart httpd
```

I can reproduce it on Linux too.

For @Pomax: a workaround is to configure the podman machine to be rootful (`podman machine set --rootful`). This may not be an option for your use case but it's worth mentioning.

For us: I am removing the `windows` and `remote` labels, adding `pasta`. This looks like a `pasta` issue and may be already known cc @Luap99

There is https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23737, the design implies some kind of race since pasta exists on its own (rootlessport and slirp4netns do the same so that is nothing new). 

If you just stop the container and check if the port is used what process has it bound (ss -tulpn inside the wsl would be useful). is it a race or does it never stop?


> That last one is very weird: no -t was provided, why does the error show -t 8081-8081:80-80, which isn't how you specify port bindings (because you can't specify port bindings for restart)?

That is just the pasta argument for publishing the port, see the pasta man page. It has nothing to do with the podman -t cli option.

It fails on my fedora system also most of time

```diff
diff --git a/libpod/container_internal.go b/libpod/container_internal.go
index fe33b5a5af..f166391bf0 100644
--- a/libpod/container_internal.go
+++ b/libpod/container_internal.go
@@ -1638,6 +1638,9 @@ func (c *Container) restartWithTimeout(ctx context.Context, timeout uint) (retEr
                        }
                }
        }()
+
+       time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
+
        if err := c.prepare(); err != nil {
                return err
        }
```

This makes it work "consistently", so yeah it is the race condition on the pasta exit path.
The only thing podman could do is always track the pasta pid and send an explicit SIGKILL to ensure pasta is dead before we start the new one. That always comes with its own challenges, pid reuse risk, we don't want to kill the wrong process for example. And even the kill itself would not guarantee the process is actually dead and the port free since kill is inherently async

@l0rd cheers. Unfortunately not an option, but a workaround for now (at least on my end) is a stop + start rather than single restart.

In terms of the only thing podman can do: rather than kill and then a sleep, when a container is created with an explicit port(range) binding, is "waiting for the port(s) to be released" with a `net.listen` not an option? The intention of a single restart command is that when it exits, you know the container has been restarted, the critical behaviour there is the restart, not how fast it tries to achieve that restart.

To be clear, I am not proposing the sleep is a valid fix, it is not! That was just my local verification to confirm it is a race condition.

FWIW running the container with a userns, i.e. `--userns keep-id` but or other userns mapping makes it work as well. That is simply because the userns forces a different network setup path which inverts the ordering we first create the oci runtime container then start pasta. That timing difference is enough to make it reliably work on my system though still no guarantee of course.
Technically it could be possible to force that code path always for rootless containers.
ah, gotcha!

Maybe unrelated but i have a problematic container with this behavior that gets immediate connection timeouts until i restart it (restart always params set)

podman restart searxng
Error: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Failed to bind port 8081 (Address already in use) for option '-t 8081-8081:8080-8080'
That looks like exactly the same error.

This should now have a robust workaround in passt [2026_01_20.386b5f5](https://archives.passt.top/passt-user/20260121055331.64707ef8@elisabeth/) and matching Fedora 42 [update](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2026-ec35ff5ffb), see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27901#issuecomment-3774246685 for details.

The underlying theoretical problem is not fixed, yet. But given that we already have https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23737, should we close this anyway?
Only if it's fixed on all operating systems? If it's fixed, the issue's resolved. if there are still targets left, the issue's not been resolved yet I'd say.

> Only if it's fixed on all operating systems? If it's fixed, the issue's resolved. if there are still targets left, the issue's not been resolved yet I'd say.

Strictly speaking, it's not fixed at all, but there's a change (now available on quite a few distributions) that should hide it reliably.

But that was not my point. My point is that this is a duplicate of #23737, as far as I understand. If it's not, let's keep this open, otherwise let's keep a single one open I'd say.

yes let's close this as duplicate, I noticed this to late and should have closed this earlier. The other issue has a PR attached with the discussion on how to correctly address this problem.

That said as @sbrivio-rh pointed out the latest pasta release should hide that issue basically as the timing changed to no longer trigger the race. Well at least our our test systems.
